segments.text: If you perhaps want to just pop your
video on.
Looks like it's on at my end, so perhaps...
Here we go. Oh, no. It disappeared again.
There we are, hopefully.
Hmm. Hmm. We can't see you, but we can hear you.
We can hear you, Brahman, but we can't see you, so,
if you would like to start. Catherine, I'll just start, and
I greatly appreciate the opportunity to speak.
I'm a professor of political science at UC.
Aside from time in private practice and in broadcasting, I've worked for 30
years at Lincoln University and at Canterbury in environmental policy
and public policy, and I've held fellowships in climate at
the Tyndall Centre in East Anglia,
at the University of Surrey in environmental engineering, at the
University of Oxford in public policy, and served for 10 years on the IPCC,
particularly in cities.
I am really worried about this bill, and
I want to stress four points.
First, I want to make the point clearly that as far as I can
understand, Cabinet was presented with incorrect information in its
budget sensitive planning,
and infrastructure material at point
43. Cabinet was assured that while some
stakeholders might be concerned for the
dilution of environmental protections or hindering
growth,
this merger will improve
delivery of policy and that other countries have successfully integrated
similar policies. I literally can't find any evidence
of other countries that have successfully done this, particularly
such diverse portfolios as housing, urban development,
along with transport, environment, and infrastructure.
The only two that I can find are Kosovo and the
Maldives, and both are struggling.
The countries that we like to compare ourselves to merge
similar functions. So the UK, for instance, merges
environment, food, and rural affairs.
Australia merges agriculture, environment, energy, and water.
Singapore merges sustainability and environment, and Canada,
environment, climate, and nature.
The two countries that have tried to merge housing and urban
issues with environment in this way or tourism as well,
Kosovo and the Maldives, have had huge problems of
conflicts of interest, and I noticed that the Parliamentary
Secretary for Environment will make this point later.
But I would really just like to stress that the argument that was
made to Cabinet that it's difficult to coordinate the
trade-offs currently between these key areas is
actually the fundamental point of Cabinet.
Cities themselves are responsible for about
72% of world emissions.
So I know in New Zealand we talk a lot about agriculture, but it's actually at the
cities level, we can make the biggest differences in trade-offs where we
can really make a significant change at the moment, particularly in areas like
transport.
And it's also
in cities where our three major cities are all struggling at the moment
with key issues of infrastructure around water and sewerage.
Now, it is vital that we have a clear and
transparent process by which the ministers of
environment
and the ministers regarding cities or regarding
climate,
and have those trade-offs made explicit to the public.
For Cabinet to receive papers from a mega ministry where those
decisions have been resolved internally is to reduce the
accountability and the oversight of Cabinet itself.
I don't say this lightly. This is reckless.
My second key point is that going slower will take
us further.
I was witness to the first cross-party
consensus between 1986 and
when the then Simon Upton, Geoffrey Palmer,
ministers and government officials from both National and Labor
worked collaboratively together.
It was a long and involved process, and it went across two elected
governments. But it was a process where everybody was focused
on getting the best out of this for New Zealand, and I really want to
appeal to that spirit again.
Cabinet was urged
and promised that urgency wouldn't be used in the House
to pass this piece of legislation.
Yet I see that yesterday the decision was already
made to appoint a secretary, and it
isn't just a secretary for environment, it's a secretary for environment,
for cities,
and for regional development. Now, that decision's already made, so why
are we even consulting?
Oh, are you there?
Yes, I am. Just pausing because I'm so exasperated.
We were also told that there would be a statutory
office for the Secretary of the Environment.
And
Simon Upton in his PCE role, and I
must admit I, was at least hopeful.
But when we look at the criteria,
the last time we made one of these appointments in 2022, the criteria was
set out for being strategic environmental lead.
And this is so important when environment is fundamental to our
economy, it's fundamental to our major sectors, it's fundamental
to iwi, it's fundamental to future
generations.This time, it appears that
the key priorities for the
secretary are also to be secretary not only for environment,
but now in the new job description, for cities and for
regional
development. This does simply dilute and confuse the role
of the Secretary for Environment.
And finally, the actual legislation itself does not defend the
intrinsic quality and value of ecosystems.
It doesn't defend and protect the quality environment.
It's not clear how the treaty principles will be
upheld,
and it's not clear how the needs of future generations will be protected.
This is rushed and poor legislation, and I'm really
disappointed. Thank you.
Thank you very much, Bronwyn, for your submission.
I'm just going to open it up for some questions.
Rachel Brooking.
A very quick question. Thank you, Bronwyn, for all the work that's gone
into the submission and for alerting us to international
examples. My question is not about the international examples, it's just about that
last point around the intrinsic values of ecosystems
and the future generation point, as well as the treaty point.
Do you
see a way through that could include those
issues in this mega ministry,
and
would your concerns remain if those provisions were put
into legislation somewhere?
The concerns would remain because at the moment we've conflated those
secretarial roles.
The first bit, as proposed, was that there would be a
Secretary of Environment, but we need, as also Simon Upton's
made really clear, we need to make those trade-offs absolutely
clear,
and at the moment it's not. And at the moment, we
are not clear that we will have somebody who will be able to
lead and advance the intrinsic values and the strategy that's
needed for ecosystems, for the quality of environment, and for the
needs of future generations
or treaty principles in that role, while they're also trying to balance
the needs of local government and regional government.
It's a mess,
and there are huge transactional costs for making these kind of
changes. So we also need to know not just what government's going to do, but I
think the public has a right to know what the opposition would do in this situation
as well. This is really important.
These are core to our economy. They are core to the future of the
country, and to make this decision in this rushed
way, to have already made an appointment yesterday, is really
poor governance.
Look, thank you very much, Bronwyn, for your submission and the time
that you've put into that and this morning. So thank you. You have a good day.
Thanks, Catherine, and I hope that you can turn this around, and I really
sincerely mean that, because future generations, and even
our textbooks that we're currently writing for current students, whether they're
blue-greens or red-greens, I don't care, it
really matters for the society and for our future that we get this right.
Thank you, Bronwyn.
So we will now move to our next submitter, who is here with us in person this
morning, the New Zealand Forest Owners Association.
Welcome to the select committee. You have 10 minutes with us, so we will
hand over to you to open up, and then we'll open up for questions.
Thanks, Catherine. Kia ora koutou. Ko Elizabeth Hague toku ingoa.
I'm the CEO of Forest Owners Association, and I'm joined today by
Rachel Miller, who's our environment manager.
Forest Owners Association represents plantation forest growers,
so timber producers in New Zealand.
And our members make up about
1.3 million hectares of the 1.8 million hectares
of New Zealand plantation forest.
I will pass over to Rachel to outline our concerns with the current
proposal.
Okay. Because forestry sits at the intersection of environmental
protection, land use, climate policy, and economic
development, the proposed disestablishment of the Ministry for the Environment
is of key concern to our sector.
We recognize that the government may seek efficiencies through consolidation, and
we acknowledge that merging ministries can reduce administrative
duplication.
However, we have key concerns about the proposal to absorb
MFE into a much larger MCERT ministry.
Our core concern is around the risk that essential environmental
policy functions will be diluted within a ministry that has strong
development, transport, and urban growth mandate.
Our concerns are focused on two key issues,
conflict of functions and secondly, dilution of environmental
capability.
Additionally, while we are reviewing the option for an MCERT,
we would like to highlight the need for some better
cross-agency collaboration through our experiences in the past.
Right, on the first issue,
the Ministry for the Environment's role is deliberately limited to policy,
standards, and advice on the management of natural and physical resources.
Importantly, MFE does not carry out development or delivery
activities.
By contrast, the Ministries of Transport, Housing, and Urban Development are
directly involved in infrastructure development, urban expansion, and
implementation of decisions that can affect land, water, biodiversity, and
emissions. So merging these different roles into a single
ministry creates an inherent risk of conflict of interest.
We are particularly concerned about the proposed amendment to Section 32 of the
Environment Act, requiring the secretary to consider environmental
outcomes only as far as practical
From our perspective, this wording signals that environmental
considerations may become secondary to development priorities
rather than sitting as an independent and authoritative policy voice.
For forestry, it's critical. Environmental rules shape where trees
can grow, how forests are managed, how carbon
sequestration is recognized. Those rules must be
developed free from pressure to prioritize short-term development outcomes.
So our second key concern is on the risk of dilution.
So MFA is relatively small compared to the large operationally
focused agencies
that it's proposed to merge with. There's a real risk that MFA's
specialist expertise in climate policy, environmental monitoring,
and systems
thinking will simply be overwhelmed.
We've seen this before. So the forestry sector has lived through the
dissolution of the NZ Forest Service in 1987, and then
later the merger of the Ministry
of Forestry into MPI in 1998.
Each time, forestry's policy capability became a small part of a
much larger ministry competing for attention and funding.
This has resulted in reduced visibility of
forestry-specific issues, difficulty maintaining specialist
expertise, and environmental considerations being crowded
by higher-profile priorities. We are concerned that the same
pattern could occur here, with environmental policy funding and focus eroding over
time once it is pulled into a broad mega-ministry.
So while we are reviewing the MCERT, we wanted to take the
opportunity to highlight another issue that we have had.
So,
that it relates to cross-agency collaboration between MFA
and the Ministry for Primary Industries.
In forestry, the technical and operational expertise sits within
MPI,
yet MFA remains the ultimate gatekeeper for forestry regulation.
In the past, policy has been developed by MFA without significant
integration of forestry expertise.
The proposal for MCERT is a timely opportunity to strengthen this function.
In closing,
we urge the committee to proceed
with caution. Environmental policy requires independence,
specialist expertise, and a clear mandate.
We believe these qualities are at risk under the proposed merger.
We respectfully recommend retaining a strong standalone environmental policy
function while using the reform process to strengthen collaboration.
Thanks for the opportunity to speak, and we welcome some questions.
Well, thank you for your submission. We'll take the first question from Rachel.
Yeah, thank you. And just going right to the end of your submission there, you're
talking about mechanisms for cross-agency collaboration.
I'm wondering if you want to expand on them,
what you see could work. And also,
the question is, I guess you're referring to MPI as an
example in terms of its relationship with MFA, and that you
can't just add all the
different issues into one giant department.
You've always got to put a line somewhere.
And so it's important that wherever that line is, that those agencies can
still talk to each other, even if there's a line. Is that a fair summary?
Yeah, correct.
Yeah. And so what are some examples, then, for good ways for those
agencies to work together?
Yeah, I think we've had a few examples
over the years where things have worked well, and like
with any policy function, things can kind of wax and wane over
time. So there have been times, like in the development of ETS
policy, where I think we felt like things have worked a bit better.
And certainly through the
reforms in 2018, it felt like there was some good kind
of cross-collaboration and perhaps a little more recognition
given to the importance of forestry.
And then I think over time, those things have
kind of waxed and waned. So in the creation of any ministry, we're just
looking to see more of that,
I guess the separation of functions so that
as folks that are working on the ground, that we can see clearly kind of
who we're working with and how their functions are separated.
So to your point,
particularly for our stakeholders, we need to be able to point to where the
clear, bright lines are around
different functions. But also, I think as agencies
work together, it's really important to us that that operational
understanding can come through into the policy functions.
And I guess this is the key risk that we see with the
amalgamation of the Ministry for the Environment into MCERT,
is that the environmental policy function, as we've
experienced it, does need a grounding and a connection to the operational
function. But I guess the concern is if it's getting more connected into
development than into the primary sector, there's a real risk of urban bias
and a move away from the kind of rural operational input that
we would be seeking from the forestry sector.
Is there anything you want to add to that, Rachel?
Sounds good.
Yep.
Thank you. And we'll take one last question from Lan.
Tena koutou. Thank you for your submission.
I wanted to pick up on your points about conflict
of functions, and you say that there is a risk
of conflict of interest which has not been considered under the bill,
and you're saying that there is a section in the bill that appears to
deal with it as far as practical, but that this might
not actually address that. Do you want to expand on your concerns there?
Well, I think just the phrase "as far as practicable" to us suggests a
prioritization between development and environmental
considerations.
Mm-hmm.
And again, something I guess that
it comes back to this, some people call it the
rural-urban divide, but I think we've got genuine concerns about
how natural infrastructure and built infrastructure
are regarded in terms of development overall.
And I know
Over time, we're going to have to face some conflicts and difficult trade-offs,
particularly with climate change in that space.
But I think out of the gate, to see what looks seemingly in the bill like a
prioritization of development over environment right out of the
gate suggests to us that that's not an equal
trade-off that's being considered, but it's possibly already being prioritized
through the construction of the legislation.
Mm-hmm.
We're concerned that funding for infrastructure will take precedence over funding
for the environment and ecosystem services, et cetera.
Mm-hmm.
And I think there's been a launch at Parliament recently of the natural
infrastructure plan by the Aotearoa Circle.
I just want to point to the fact that in a lot of ways, we're talking about just
different forms of infrastructure here, natural infrastructure and built
infrastructure. And I think over time, we're going to find more and better ways to
integrate those things. But if we kind of prioritize one over the other,
it probably isn't going to help that integration.
Look, thank you very much for your time today.
We have met time, so we appreciate your submission. Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you very much.
Thank you.
So we are now just going to skip forward to
EcoSolutions, who I understand are with us
already online.
This is a five-minute one?
Yes.
It
is.
Madam Chair,
Alyssa is also online.
Morena.
All names.
Morena. Good morning.
Welcome to the Select Committee this morning.
You have five minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you.
Kia ora. EcoSolutions has received valuable mentoring and capacity
building through the waste minimization funding for projects based in Te
Tai Tokerau.
And I would like to address you in that
capacity. If I had more time, I'd ask you why the government believes
disestablishing the Ministry for Environment is such a good idea.
If the goal was better coordination or efficiency, that can be achieved without
dismantling MFE. Structural reform does not require wiping out an
independent institution. This bill removes government's ability
to ensure environment has an independent system-wide voice
at a time when land, water and climate we all rely on are under increasing
pressure. It weakens environmental governance, undermines the Treaty of Waitangi
obligations and creates legal risk for the Crown.
Second, on the evidence and risk, folding environmental funding functions into
a development-led mega ministry creates clear conflicts of interest, as
has been clearly stated. And international
experience shows that in mega ministries, environmental
outcomes are pushed aside, and when the environmental decisions are legally
flawed, the result is not speed or savings.
It's court cases, delays, uncertainty for councils and developers and higher
costs for taxpayers.
Thirdly, Te Tiriti matters. The Treaty of Waitangi
requires partnership, active protection, and rangatiratanga.
These obligations cannot be simply shifted on paper.
MFE has built relationships and skills that support Maori and
community involvement in environmental decision-making.
And to all Maori, decisions are made for future generations.
Weakening environmental institutions today shifts risk and cost
to our mokopuna. It's not fair, it's not good public finance.
Fourth, accountability. The bill weakens our clear responsibility and
reduces transparency. When accountability is spread thin,
failures are harder to spot and fix.
This increases legal challenges and costs. Why would we choose that?
Finally, there's a better option.
If a mega ministry is needed, why would you choose such odd
bedfellows? A mega
ministry of built environment and infrastructure
ministry, combining the Ministry of Housing and Urban Development, Ministry of
Transport, local government functions of the Department of Internal Affairs,
and the Ministry of Business Innovation and Employment, for example,
would combine portfolios that share a common purpose towards
growth, delivery and implementation, allowing clear delivery,
accountability and fewer internal contradictions.
And critically, the environmental regulation remains independent
from development pressures, as in countries like Australia, Canada and
the UK. This protects integrity and trust.
In conclusion, this is not just poor policy.
This is a legal and governance risk.
This also demonstrates a lack of democratic process.
There is no remit to demolish the environmental overview.
Instead, the MFE should be combined with the Ministry of Regulation, for
example, allowing oversight over all policy.
But any move should be more robustly thought out with clear
justifications. Sound decision-making means strong, independent
environmental oversight. Honoring Te Tiriti in
2026 means protecting those institutions, not
dismantling them. For legal certainty, accountability and long-term
resilience, the Ministry for Environment should be retained and strengthened,
not just disestablished. Thank you for your time.
Any questions?
Look, thank you very much, Jo. It doesn't appear we have any
questions, but we have almost met time, so we thank you for your very
passionate submission there this morning.
Appreciate your time.
Thank you.
Thanks. Thank you.
Kia ora.
And keep up your good waste minimization work.
Now we'll move on now to
Alyssa
McElverey Howard, who is also joining
us via Zoom this morning.
Oh, there we go. We can see you there.
Might just need to turn on your microphone, because we can't hear
you
Can you hear me?
There we go. Yes, we can hear and see you.
Welcome to the select committee this morning.
You have five minutes with us, so we will hand it over to you to talk us through
your submission.
Okay. Really appreciate this opportunity.
So it's my understanding that the purpose of this
legislation, which removes New Zealand's Ministry of Environment,
placing it on lesser footing within a mega-ministry,
is to streamline economic growth.
Growth within this context does not include investment
in the life-giving environmental resources, but in
enabling investment in technology and infrastructure,
which is costly across the board but rarely lives up
to the hype. My concern is that buried within a melange
of other departments and priorities, protections which
exist to support environmental health and wider living
systems will be eroded by stealth.
If this appears a cynical response, as a scientist and environmental
advocate, I have watched the great promise for a glowing
future made by this coalition, while at the same
time token funding those things on which life
depends, feeding corporate takeovers, whose priority
is to distribute proceeds to shareholders rather than
contributing to and edifying the wider resource
and enabling
unsustainable extraction in food production,
while leaving the environment poorer and many people
struggling to pay for food.
This government has undermined environmental protection in the
following ways: by reversing climate action, emissions
reductions through reinstating of offshore oil
and gas exploration, weakening methane targets,
and the delaying of the inclusion of agricultural emissions.
By passing the fast-track legislation which enables
existing environmental protections to be bypassed
for the financial benefit of a small sector of the economy.
By undermining fresh water protections to give commercial users
priority over ecosystem health, by repealing the clean
car discount, by cutting funding to public transport,
in prioritizing the buildings of roads and motorways, by
cutting funding for climate research, and by removing
sustainability as a core principle.
It's hard from the subset of government actions to have
faith that this new ministry will continue to protect the
environment, which is our fundamental resource.
We have no powerful advocate for the environment within
government coalition, even though all our human
activities are within the context of environment, and
this needs to change. As well as providing the resources on
what life depends, environmental quality is
profoundly grounding for human wellbeing and is an
important component for human mental health.
The expansion of technology and information packaged
to manipulate and grab attention is leading to
a more anxious world, and our respect for the
environment has never been more important. Thank you.
Any questions?
Thank you very much for your submission.
We've got one question from Lan.
Kia ora, Elsa.
Mm-hmm.
Sorry, can you hear me?
I can. Can you-
Oh, lovely. Kia ora. Yeah, thank you so much for your submission this
morning.
We've just heard from-- I'm not sure if you heard the previous submitters,
but they've got real concerns about the sort of
transparency of
basically decisions being made at the ministry level before
advice, for example, even gets to Cabinet.
Do you have any concerns as a citizen about that
transparency aspect?
I think as I've followed actually what's going on, I've really tried to follow it.
What we actually see is we see a profound impact of lobbying,
and I would have personal concerns for this because actually we're
looking at there is
this undermining of
privatization that's going on, and actually, the
problem with this approach is this whole value
to shareholders thing.
And it's not cohesive for
society and it
benefits a few people.
This is like the environment is something that we all own, we all contain,
and everything exists within this.
However, there's a lot of lobbyists to ask for pieces
of the pie,
and what is really concerning is that the talks
about royalties and so on, and the spin to
talk about
limiting our environmental damage,
it's so
without integrity or
we cannot
scientifically prove that. It's just if you say something, if you say it
loud enough, often enough, and if you say it in Parliament, it has more
clout,
apparently.
Thank you very much, Elsa. We have met time.
We appreciate your submission today. So thank you very much.
Good luck to you all, and I really appreciate your
public service. Thank you.
Thank you.
Now we are moving to our next submitter, McGinnis
Institute, who I can see has joined us already.
There we go. Josie, welcome to the select committee this morning.
So you have 10 minutes with us. So we'll hand it over to you for
some opening remarks and then open up for questions
Thank you very much, and thanks all of you. Good morning.
Wendy is sick today, so just I'm stepping in.
And if you have any questions, I'll try to answer, but just bear in mind
that
we might need to come back to you.
So thanks for your patience with that.
I'll keep it quick because you've already heard a lot of good points this morning,
and you'll hear them today.
But as a background, the institute's focus is on designing policy for the
long-term good of New Zealand.
And so there's definitely a need to improve our planning
regime. However, the bill in this current form definitely
has a few issues.
And we're concerned, like many others, that the bill focuses on the
short term, meaning long term and environmental policy will
lose out in favor of short term, more economic
gains.
So as you know, MFA's been in operation since
1986. And it was established for a special
purpose,
and independent ministries really make a difference.
So this bill, we're struggling to understand the rationale for
this mega ministry.
We're concerned that it leads to inconsistency,
uncertainty, and increased costs for public and private
sectors. So people and businesses are struggling to
plan because they don't know what changes are happening, and
there's just a real risk here that the next government
or the one after that will change it back, and that's really difficult
for people to try and make a business or plan their
lives, really.
There's-
Oh, yeah.
It was a sneeze. Don't worry.
Bless you.
So
I guess, yeah, we're lacking evidence on where these mega ministries have
worked internationally, so we'd love to see some analysis
of how this works before we implement it.
Obviously, this bill removes kind of that purpose of
protection for the environment.
It's creating this ministry where someone's trying to balance all these different
purposes. And I think we know what ones will
lose out. It's the ones that are less tangible and longer
term.
The bill also will rely on internal decision-making, which
is likely, as you've heard, to create conflicts of interests,
and lacks public engagement, so more likely that these critical
decisions are made behind closed doors.
And this sort of weakens independent,
transparent oversight for environmental legislation.
So I also want to talk about
the Government Department Strategies Index.
So this is something the institute does
every few years, and we look at government department strategies across
all of government and rank them.
And so in this, looking at this proposed
bill using these government department strategies,
there's a few implications. So one is that the bill will
combine government department strategies from four different sectors.
The other is that the proposal will have significant
implications to the budgeting process.
Groupings of sectors have been used in the past to indicate
how public funds are designated
and sets the scenes for accountability.
So combining these four sectors is quite unusual and a bit
difficult for accountability.
And the second is that this intention will
create a super ministry, possibly with 28 GDSS to
manage,
which is a lot. For context, MB has
16 government department strategies to manage.
So key implications from this work, I can send the Excel sheet
that Wendy's prepared and a bit more detail if you'd
like. But that the first one is that the
proposal will require changes in legislation.
The second is that the opportunities for integration of strategies are
unclear, particularly given the process that already
exists for departments to work together on issues such as climate.
And it's hard to see what strategies would benefit from integration.
So to kind of conclude,
to build the economy and society as a whole,
this conversation is really important.
So we're really grateful that you guys are doing it, thank you, and taking up your
time.
But
a new resource management and planning policy should really focus
on providing three benefits. So the first is
certainty for businesses and citizens.
And everyone,
we've talked to people in the private sector and public, and there's a
lot of uncertainty in the world right now.
And then also uncertainty with what's
happening
which is natural, but if we can provide some certainty in terms of
planning, I think that will really help.
The second is a strategic approach.
So a bit more detailed analysis to
understand how MFA is benefiting or
harming local communities and how public policy around this can
improve outcomes for New Zealanders.
And
the third is trying to develop a nonpartisan
approach. So bringing everyone on the journey together,
even though different goals,
but there are some things we can agree on.
We want our kids to be able to go swimming and fishing.
And so protecting the environment is a really important goal
for New Zealand.And in this context, this
bill doesn't really support that or make
sense. And yeah, I'll finish up
there. You've got a bit of a day ahead of you, so I'm very
grateful and thank you very much.
Thank you, Josie, and thank you for stepping in last minute as well.
We do have a question from Lan.
Tena koe, Josie. Thank you so much for your submission.
I wanted to pick up on your really clear point from the
institute that you say the current system in terms of MFE has
been designed for all New Zealanders current and future, whereas the
new system is designed for a small number of parties focused on
meeting their short-term profits, and that many of those
who actually benefit from this bill are not New Zealand companies or New Zealand
residents. Can you expand about why you've put that point in
there?
Yeah, that's a great question. So I'm not sure if you're
aware, the institute's done a lot of work in aquaculture,
especially in the Marlborough Sounds, with salmon farming in particular.
So I think that's a really good example of that
point, I suppose, where
these companies,
for New Zealand King Salmon, for example, is owned by
Malaysian shareholders, a mix of different shareholders, but
that's one. And so
the bill, how it's changing is sort
of going to benefit these companies who operate in New
Zealand, but actually the benefits at
the end of the day are going
away internationally.
Mm-hmm.
Does that sort of make sense?
Yeah, no, thank you so much for that example. It's really clear.
Yeah. So there's lots of examples, but I think that's a good one.
Thank you, Josie, for your time today.
We have met time, so
yeah, thank you for your submission and for filling in this morning.
Yeah.
Hope your day gets better.
Yes.
Yeah. Thanks for your patience, guys. Have a great day.
Thank you.
Thank you. So we now move on to The Rubbish Trip.
And we have Hannah with us. Welcome to the select committee this
morning. You have five minutes with us, Hannah, so we'll hand it over
to you for your submission.
Kia ora. Kia ora, can you hear me?
Yes.

considerations are not lost or overridden at the management level.
In this model, political leadership charts the course between competing

Zealand at the moment. Sweden bundled their environmental ministry
in with energy business and industry in 2022.

environment in 2020, and bundling protection and
production in together didn't magically align the two separate drivers.
